concept:
Tested 4 ? The most common causes of fever of unknown origin (FUO) are:
Infection
Cancer
Autoimmune disease
Four signs and symptoms of streptococcal pharyngitis are:
Fever
Pharyngeal erythema
Tonsillar exudate
Lack of cough
Nonsuppurative complication of streptococcal infection that is not altered by treatment of primary infection is: Postinfectious gloumerulonephritis
Asplenic patients are particularly susceptible to what organisms? Encapsulated organisms:
Pneumococcus
Meningococcus
Haemophilus influenzae
Klebsiella
Number of bacterial culture on clean-catch specimen to diagnose a UTI is? 10^5 bacterial/mL
Which healthy population is susceptible to UTIs? Pregnant women (treat this group aggressively due to potential complications)
Patient from California or Arizona presents with fever, malaise, cough and night sweats. Diagnosis? Treatment?
Diagnosis:Coccidioidomycosis
Treatment:Fluconazole
What is non-painful chancre? Primary syphilis
The "blueberry-muffin" rash is characteristic of what congenital infection? Rubella
When neonates present with meningitis, what are the causes? Treatment?
Causes:
Group B strep
E. coli
Listeria
Treatment: Treat with gentamicin and ampicillin
When infants present with meningitis, what are the causes? Treatment?
Causes:
Pneumococcus
Meningococcus
H. influenzae
Treatment: Treat with cefotaxime and vancomycin
What should always be done prior to a lumbar puncture (LP)?
Check for increased intracrainial pressure (ICP)
Look for papilledema
With CSF findings,
low glucose
PMN predominance indicate: Bacterial meningitis
With CSF findings,
normal glucose
lymphocytic predominance indicate: Aseptic (viral) meningitis
Numerous RBCs in serial CSF sample indicate: Subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH)
With CSF findings, increased gammaglobulins indicate: Multiple sclerosis
A patient initially presents with a pruritic papule with regional lymphadenopathy which evolves into black eschar after 7 to 10 days. Diagnosis? Treatment?
Diagnosis:Anthrax
Treatment:Ampicillin
Findings in tertiary syphilis are:
Tabes dorsalis
General paresis
Gummas
Argyll-Robertson pupil
Aortitis
Aortic root aneurysms
Where is lyme region endemic? North American north east
Characteristics of secondary lyme disease are:
Arthralgias
Migratory polyarthropathies
Bell's palsy
Myocarditis
Cold agglutinins are: Mycoplasma
A 24 year-old male presents with soft white plaques on his tongue and the back of his throat. Diagnosis? Workup?
Diagnosis:Candidal thrush
Work-up:Include HIV test
At what CD4 count would you begin Pneumocystis carinii (PCP) prophylaxis in an HIV positive patient? What count would you begin for the MAC prophylaxis?
Count:Less than 201 for PCP (with TMP)
MAC prophylaxis: Less than 101 (with clarithromycin/azithromycin)
When is the neutropenic nadir post chemo-therapy? 7 to 10 days
What is the treatment for HSV meningitis? Acyclovir
What is the antigen test to confirm the diagnosis of Rocky Mountain spotted fever rickettsial disease? Weil-Felix test
Erythema migrans Lesion of primary lyme disease
What are the classic physical findings for endocarditis?
Fever
Heart murmur
Osler's nodes
Splinter hemorrhages
Janeway lesions
Roth's spots
What is the most common infection from a blood transfusion? HCV
What organism has branching rods in an oral infection? Actinomyces israelii
What organism produces a painful chancroid? Haemophilus ducreyi
What organism often produces an infection after a dog or cat bite? Pasteurella multocida
What organism produces meningitis in adults? Neisseria meningitidis
What organism produces meningitis in the elderly? Streptococcus pneumoniae
What organism commonly infects alcoholics with pneumonia? Klebsiella
What organism makes the patient's sputum resemble currant jelly? Klebsiella
What organism commonly causes infection in burn victims? Pseudomonas
What organism often produces osteomyelitis from a foot wound puncture? Pseudomonas
What organism often produces osteomyelitis in sickle cell patients? Salmonella
